text.content:
Book of the Wider World — p.382-395

In the time of the Makers (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.WNfxD0xaxSCvrl2E]{264}), the Stone Lords ruled the Steplands (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.DBEagy54rlf52AOz]{372}), the southern stretch of the Huffel Peaks (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.VAh2hWUYrYMaVuVJ]{236}), and the South Manmarch (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.hx1EiGo418JBGmVD]{350}). They carved cities out of the hills. They raised towering monuments. They sang stone, crystal, and glass into impossible shapes.

The Stone Lords’ empire was grand, but it was not all glory. It was built on the backs of countless human servants. And while the Stone Lords were not especially cruel, neither were they kind. They imposed order on their world, by whatever means necessary.

For, better than the other Makers, the Stone Lords knew the Things Below (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.HurhcWKZBn60Vcq3]{412}). Knew the insidious songs that echoed in the deep. Knew the chaos promised. Knew that they must never, ever be freed.

And so, when the Green Lords (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.gnP4Q10EDePdvgqz]{210}) called on the Things Below to put down their own servants’ rebellion, the Stone Lords turned on their brethren. They shattered the Green Lords’ lands and hunted down the survivors.

Yet: the seals had cracked. The Things Below sang all the louder. The Stone Lords themselves stood resolute, but their servants did not. Corruption seeped through their society like water finding cracks in a poorly laid foundation.

The end came swiftly. Sorcerers turned the city of Dulenn into a massive sinkhole, creating what is now Blackwater Lake (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.83TJVo6wM5U4TDgM]{48}). Seals were broken across the land and horrors slithered up through the tunnels. Mobs turned on their former masters. One by one, the domains and cities of the Stone Lords fell into ruin. The Time of Cataclysm (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.HurhcWKZBn60Vcq3]{413}) had arrived.

Names

Breton- and Cornish-inspired, but with extra vowels for a drawn-out sound: Aenedellion, Caedour, Daenizel, Eelowain, Gaeille, Iaves, Kaeteal, Loiawen, Mearauwd, Neassa, Oaneaz, Paerrain, Rozainne, Saennaen, Taenguey, Wainoac, Yainniac

Combine with prefixes like “Aalz” (“the great”), “Skol” (“master”), or “Loaji” (“apprentice”), etc.

Hooks

  • Someone claims to have found a Stone Lord ruin abundant with precious gems
  • A petrified prisoner of the Stone Lords was recently reanimated, and must now make sense of this new age
  • While seeking shelter from the elements, the PCs stumble on a Stone Lord ruin
  • Hillfolk need help dealing with dangerous stone constructs that are running amok

Themes

Pick, or roll 1d12. 1d12 theme

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.RsLaKWnpQ7xGwIQt]{1d12}Themes

1Rock and stone: excavated and shaped into grandiose structures
2Crystals/gems/makerglass (page 388): decorative, practical, or thrumming with power
3Songs/resonance/vibrations/echoes: to empower, shape, transform, or destroy
4Seals/bindings/things trapped in stone: creatures/spirits/ideas/ memories/free will
5Constructs/stone given life, or an approximation thereof
6Authority/order/hierarchy/tradition
7Human servants (slaves?): their corruption by the Things Below (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.HurhcWKZBn60Vcq3]{412}), their revolt, the Barrow Builders (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.Qir4rs4BUipTSyFQ]{34}) they became
8Bonds broken/seals opened/collapse and chaos/the Things Below set loose
9-12Sized for giants, and roll 1d8 again

Sites

Pick, or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.hgDTjOKkjdeGNDCy]{1d6}Site

1-3Lingering signs of their presence and influence (see below)
4-6A ruin (page 386)

Lingering signs

Pick, or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.0otc9A2I0zv06lL7]{1d6}Sign

1Something trapped in stone, crystal, or makerglass (next column)
2Strange geologies (next page)
3-4Fragments of old construction (stairs, walls, foundations, chimneys), all that’s left of once-grand structures
5A stretch of the Makers’ Roads (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.FEIURyGVNdxdU9vY]{270}), intact or broken but unearthed/barely visible
6A monument, marker, or enigma (next page)

Something trapped

For example:

  • Someone trapped in a stalagmite, their grasping hands, anguished face, and mournful moans just barely discernible
  • A hollow, seamless cube of rune-carved white stone, 6 feet per side, bitterly cold; a dozen specters (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.sNLYfvJEFM1v463y]{79}) are sealed within, impotent and mad
  • A stretch of shining black flagstones, showing reflections of people long past; those who open their minds see a parade of memories, clear as day
  • A construct (page 392) with a crack in its “head,” possessed by an evil spirit; bound in a circle of deeply-cut runes
  • A labyrinth of stone walls, the walkways forming a rune of binding, some sort of demon trapped in the center

Strange geologies

For example:

  • A large grotto, unnaturally smooth and precise, like part of a sphere just vanished from the stone
  • A gorge cutting perpendicularly through a valley, the walls lined with glass
  • A tunnel or valley, shaped so that the smallest sounds echo echo echo
  • A cliff face, the striations perfectly smooth and parallel, the different strata strikingly different colors or types of stone
  • A slope entirely made of smooth, rounded stone; like an old lava flow, but chert, granite, or limestone
  • An enormous rock formation, shot through with dozens of small holes that catch the wind and sing a haunting melody.

Monument, marker, or enigma

For example:

  • A crater of bare stone, 100 paces across, with a lozenge of black stone floating in the center, thrumming
  • A statue carved from a mountainside, fragments of its stone sword littering the valley below
  • A pedestal bearing two vast and trunkless legs of stone; hints of an inscription, now unreadable
  • Giant stone heads arrayed upon a slope, their faces faded yet still disapproving
  • A pylon of glass, 100 feet tall at least (and who knows how deeply set into the earth), faintly humming, faintly glowing
  • Great lines gouged into the earth, running for miles; meaningless from the surface, but making enormous runes when seen from on high
  • A large room in some cave or ruin, the charred bones of a giant at the center; bits of blackened bone/tooth/carapace litter the edge; hundreds of fist-sized shards of crystal are embedded in the walls
  • A chamber with dozens of glass cylinders, fixed vertically against a wall, each of differing length and width—some sort of instrument, perhaps?

Ruin

Pick or roll for themes (page 383), structure, purpose, architectural elements, and condition. Place the ruin in an appropriate terrain (see page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.VAh2hWUYrYMaVuVJ]{236} for the Huffel Peaks, page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.DBEagy54rlf52AOz]{372} for the Steplands, or page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.hx1EiGo418JBGmVD]{350} for the South Manmarch). Populate it with lingering signs (page 384), artifacts (page 388) and/or dangers of the Stone Lords (page 390) or the appropriate region.

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.GTEfviNPJNlY6Tuc]{1d6}Structure

1Tightly packed, human-sized structures/rooms
2Single, bunker-like building
3Large, open space
4Tower, rising/jutting out from a hill/mountain
5Chambers/tunnels dug into the sides of cliffs/hills/mountains
6Grandiose building of impossible construction

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.aaucjRhYEoCTKwCy]{1d6}Purpose

1Dwelling (home, barracks, dormitory, prison, etc.)
2Infrastructure (aqueduct, rampart, road, bridge, etc.)
3Storage (vault, warehouse, library, museum, etc.)
4Production (workshop, factory, mine, farm, etc.)
5Esoteric (laboratory, experiment, power source, etc.)
6Civic life (agora, theater, shrine, monument, tomb, etc.)

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.XADDMT2FxoYfMceI]{1d6}Architectural elements

1Great slabs of marble/granite/limestone/etc., far too big for humans to lift
2Seamless stone structures/tunnels, like they were poured or molded that way
3Runes carved into stone and shaped out of walls, roads, tunnels, etc.
4Crystals/makerglass: catching light/ wind, humming/glowing, chiming/ gonging
5Stones/crystals that emanate emotion/mood/thoughts/memories
6Grandiosity of scale, big enough to humble even the Makers

Most Maker-ruins are sized for giants— people two or even three times as tall as humans—but often with smaller-scale rooms, passages, and so forth for their servants.

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.NY2WM7rHzgEnXfyg]{1d6}Condition

1-2Shattered and laid waste by time, war, or vandalism; mostly buried, overgrown, collapsed; its purpose unrecognizable
3-4Crumbling, largely buried, overgrown, or hidden away
5Buried, overgrown, or hidden, but mostly intact
6Fully visible and strangely well-preserved

Many Stone Lord ruins were reclaimed (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.Qir4rs4BUipTSyFQ]{36}) by the Barrow Builders at some point. See Blackwater Lake (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.83TJVo6wM5U4TDgM]{51}) and Three Coven Lake (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.uHM6ZyjcWV4QEF22]{442}).

The PCs cut north from Three Coven Lake and seek out a place to camp. I decide that they find a Stone Lord ruin. I roll 6 for theme (authority/order/hierarchy/tradition), a 5 for structure (chambers dug into the sides of hills), a 6 for purpose (civic life), a 3 for architectural elements (crystals/makerglass), and 4 for condition (buried or hidden, mostly intact). I imagine some sort of belltower, once used to signal the hour and give order to the day.

What remains is the hint of stone stairs leading up a rocky outcrop. Near the top, a partially collapsed tunnel opens into a smooth-walled, domed chamber. A makerglass chime hangs from the ceiling, just a few inches in diameter but a hundred feet tall or more, dropping down a shaft barely wider than it is. If tapped, the chime rings painfully loudly. If struck firmly, it resonates with a thunderous, deafening noise. I think there’s also a great big bronze maul still in the chamber, and that a kleztigr (page 379) has been using this place as a den.

Makerglass

Cut or blown into wondrous shapes, in many colors and textures, unbreakable and unworkable by mortal means. Often found near Stone Lord ruins, and highly prized.

Pick or roll for form and characteristic. 1d12 form

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.hUGszQp6Jz5dc2Df]{1d12}Makerglass

1-2Enigmatic shape (chip/shard/ chunk/block/sphere/etc.), its purpose unclear
3-4Mundane object (tool/utensil/container/lantern/etc.), though perhaps not easily recognized as such
5-6Object of war (weapon/armor/ shield/helm/etc.)
7-8Art or decoration (jewel/statue/ tiles/torc/amulet/ring/etc.)
9-10Musical instrument (bell/chime/ flute/horn/etc.)
11-12Construction (door/stairway/arch/ bricks/walls/etc.), likely immobile

Consider rolling 1d6 to inform size/scale. 1d12 characteristic

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.y0M0Hct86dejcTQo]{1d12}Makerglass (2)

1-2Breathtakingly beautiful
3-4Glows/shines (roll 1d6 for scale)
5Sings/hums/thrums (roll 1d6 for scale)
6Absorbs something (heat/sound/ light/energy/vibration/etc.)
7Roll 1d6 twice
8-12Just (just) an indestructible object made of glass

Artifacts

Various treasures

  • A severed, three-fingered hand of a stone construct (Value 0), cleverly articulated but inert
  • A single makerglass arrowhead (indestructible, 2 piercing, Value 1)
  • A makerglass jar with a threaded lid, seals airtight (indestructible, Value 2)
  • A stack of engraved ceramic tiles that fit together like a jigsaw puzzle; properly assembled, the engravings form a potent ring of runes for binding demons (Value 1, or Value 3 to the right person)
  • A ring of petrified wood, set with a shining diamond (?) (Value 2, beautiful)
  • A huge bronze gong (immobile, Value 2), carved along the edge with runes of amplification
  • A makerglass sword (close, messy, indestructible, beautiful, +1 damage, 2 piercing, Value 3)
  • A fist-sized ruby (beautiful, Value 4); if placed in a fire or otherwise heated, it hums beautiful, haunting notes

A chalky potion

magical, fragile, slow

1d4 glass vials containing thick, gritty, whitish fluid. The stopper bears a mark of transformation, flesh, and stone.

When you drink the whole potion while touching stone, you transform (over a matter of minutes) into a mass of stone, similar to that which you touch. You retain awareness of your surroundings, blind but with excellent hearing.

When you attempt to end your lithic vigil, roll +CON: on a 10+, you return to your original form, rested and invigorated—regain all your HP and clear all your debilities; on a 7-9, the transformation back is slow and arduous, mark a debility; on a 6-, you are stuck until someone pours another dose of the potion over you or communes with your spirit and helps you try again.

Power-crystal

, magical, dangerous A chunk of crystal the size of a clenched fist. If charged, it thrums or crackles with power. If depleted, it fills you with a sense of emptiness and longing.

When you brandish the depleted powercrystal at a source of elemental energy, roll +CON: on a 10+, the crystal absorbs the energy fully and as safely as possible (it is now charged); on a 7-9, the crystal absorbs the energy (it’s now charged) but pick 1:

  • You are harmed by the coursing energy, taking damage as the GM sees fit
  • The power stored in the crystal is extremely unstable, likely to discharge without warning
  • The crystal cracks; henceforth, treat a 10+ on this move as a 7-9, and you can’t pick this option again.

In the hands of a skilled arcanist, a charged power-crystal can fuel or empower a magical effect (though this is dangerous). In the hands of the incautious, the reckless, or the desperate, it can split or crack, leading to a violent explosion.

A scarred slab of marble

immobile, magical

A slab of once-beautiful stone, some 30 feet tall and about half again as wide, standing in a vaulted chamber, surrounded by deeply etched runes that speak of union and protection, consumption and sacrifice. The stone bears a multitude of chips, gouges, and cracks. Dust and debris surround it.

When you pry a chip of stone from the marble and swallow it whole, while standing within the ring of runes, your flesh becomes like stone and your DEX becomes -2 until the effect ends. The next three times you would suffer physical harm, the marble slab suffers the harm instead. The effect ends after the third blow.

Minor arcana

Pick 1 or have someone roll.

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.a0QMB5EaDxkOahhw]{1d6}Minor arcanum

1A giant’s dormitory (page 509)
2@UUID[Compendium.stonetop.arcana.Item.Md3BgG5gIj7kLbMn]{Rune-etched pillars} (page 513)
3@UUID[Compendium.stonetop.arcana.Item.Xo4MrR6rTu8wWoXy]{A timeless vault} (page 518)
4@UUID[Compendium.stonetop.arcana.Item.Yp5NsS7sTv9xXpYz]{A sealed cave} (page 519)
5@UUID[Compendium.stonetop.arcana.Item.lC8AFF0FIJ2KkClm]{A makerglass chime} (page 525)
6@UUID[Compendium.stonetop.arcana.Item.mD9BGG1GJK3LlDmn]{A fine ceramic urn} (page 526)

Major arcana

These might very well be associated with the Stone Lords.

  • The @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.arcana.Item.iHawovCBsJJf4TyZ]{Mindgem} (page 548)
  • @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.arcana.Item.Iu7z09gddzRnpJRA]{Whispering Rocks} (page 550)

Dangers

Bound Entities

  • Spirits of the wild (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.HOWpYxmR8Q44qS3E]{356}), especially elementals associated with earth, stone, and terrain, yoked by runecraft to serve the Stone Lord’s needs
  • Spirits and beasts corrupted by the Things Below (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.HurhcWKZBn60Vcq3]{412}): petrified, encased in stone, or sealed away behind layers of runes
  • Would-be sorcerer-kings (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.Qir4rs4BUipTSyFQ]{34}), petrified or otherwise imprisoned before they could cause too much trouble
  • Ghosts (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.sNLYfvJEFM1v463y]{76}), mindlessly performing duties they were bound to perform in life, unaware that they could want anything else
  • Primordial entities (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.YxQdXyd9EDRJyEKv]{298}), imprisoned by elaborate workings of crystal and rune, bound to serve or else locked away for safekeeping

Unstable construction

The Stone Lords’ work has stood for centuries, and they were the cleverest architects to ever walk this world. But the problem with grand, clever architecture is that when it fails, it fails spectacularly.

  • A wall/floor/building is precariously perched, or a pillar/support/buttress supports an impossible load
  • Something creaks/teeters/sways/groans
  • Cracks form/debris tumbles/something starts to tip
  • Age-old construction starts to fall like dominoes (run!)
  • All is reduced to rubble, and dust, and death

Perilous stone

Pick or roll for form, original purpose, and peril, possibly rolling the Die of Fate for scope/scale. Consider how it fit into the daily lives of the Stone Lords or their servants—what they intended it for, how they avoided it, etc. 1d12 form

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.xubZn3rIZxcumRHH]{1d12}Perilous stone

1-2Mechanism/construction
3-5Rune(s): engraved in stone, worked into tile, formed out of architecture
6-8Crystal(s)/gem(s)/glass
9-11Strange stone
12Makerglass

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.xM1QJP5pt2X3mQga]{1d12}Original purpose

1-2Convenience/storage/infrastructure
3-4Entertainment/art/pleasure
5-6Violence/war/defense
7-8Production/power/industry
9-10Esoteric/research/worship
11-12Control/order/authority

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.NG8lqk2Q7YgFBg6z]{1d12}Peril

1Discharges energy
2Crushes/smashes/grinds
3Captures/imprisons/isolates/ separates
4Forbids/punishes certain actions
5Drains energy/emotion/memory
6Overwhelms senses/emotions
7Lures/entices/deceives
8Draws attention
9-10Roll 1d8 twice
11-12Roll 1d10, but make it grandiose— it doesn’t just capture, it petrifies; it doesn’t just grind, it reduces something to dust, etc.

Some examples:

  • Peacebond (page 519) runes, worked into roads, halls, public places
  • A glossy cube of blue-black stone that steals memories from those who touch it, playing them back for only the strongest of wills
  • A stone vault that might slip closed if folks are careless, no way to open it from the inside
  • A human-sized crystal, cracked, absorbing telluric energies, occasionally discharging them as bolts of lightning
  • A pile of shimmery sand; when someone touches it, it absorbs an imprint of their mind and forms itself into a simulacrum of them (convinced that it is the original)

Construct

The Stone Lords were masters not only of shaping stone, but also of animating it and giving it the semblance of life. Many such creations remain, though only a few still function and many of those function poorly.

Pick or roll a purpose, ability, size, power, sapience, and condition. Allow these elements to inform its physical form and construction. 1d12 purpose

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.EjMXbWp6KIktZdWo]{1d12}Construct

1-2War/destruction/violence/coercion
3-4Defense/protection/companionship
5-6Experimentation/curiosity/practice
7-8Construction/labor/convenience
9-10Something obscure, or very specific
11-12Roll 1d10 twice, combine

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.Ox8rpGuYogVqOD14]{1d12}Ability

1Nothing of note, just animated stone
2Mental influence/control
3Energy/elemental manipulation
4Cunning/alertness/special senses
5Disguise/camouflage/stealth
6Restraints/snares/subdual
7Ranged/area attack
8Transformation/multipart form/ swarm
9Prowess/mobility/speed
10Destructive/vicious weaponry
11-12Roll 1d10 twice

Stone Lord constructs are typically blind but able to navigate by sound and sensing vibrations. Almost all are highly durable (+4 HP) and animated by more than biology (another +4 HP), with at least 3 Armor.

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.D5TiwCOtZMv33TnA]{1d12}Size

1Cat-sized or smaller (tiny)
2-3Like a human child (small)
4-7Human-sized
8-10Like a horse, a cart, etc. (large)
11-12Like an elephant, or bigger (huge)

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.dx2zXBXjy6sinKOb]{1d12}Power

1-3Requires the presence of external source
4-8Stores power, but must return “home” to recharge
9-11Self-charging, but limited range/ activity
12Self-powered/indefinite charge

Power sources might include: singing crystals that broadcast energy; ingots of aetherium (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.3UhyCbr1XGkhX6SO]{404}); veins of special stone transmitting power through floors; crystals that absorb telluric currents/vibrations; spirits bound in makerglass; etc.

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.ig5GL6HdaJhqFGDa]{1d12}Sapience

1-3Puppet: requires active control by outside intellect
4-6Bug-like: can follow limited range of programs/tasks
7-10Beast-like: can take basic direction, complete some complex tasks independently
11Automaton: can handle complex tasks, learn; lacks true emotion/ creativity
12Person: fully sapient, potentially quite intelligent

Ask yourself: where does this construct’s sapience come from? Clever scripting? Spirits bound to stone? The will drained from living servants? Something else? 1d12 condition

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.WPIwzmOuGnbi8LIe]{1d12}Construct (2)

1-2No power/power source failing
3-4Broken/impaired limb(s) or chassis
5-6Trapped/pinned/buried
7-8Behaving erratically
9-10Roll 1d8 twice
11-12Fully functional
Lithic servant
Horde, construct, implacable
HP 11; Armor 3 (made of stone)
Damage stone fists d6 (hand, grabby)
Special qualities externally powered; bug-like intellect; blind; tremorsense
Instinct to follow its last orders
  • Act with eternal patience and endurance
  • Act with no sense of consequence
  • Hurt someone unintentionally while performing its tasks
Imagine one of those artists’ mannequins,
but full-sized and made of polished rock.
Two fingers and a thumb on each hand.
Faceless, expressionless, making a stone-
on-stone grinding noise as they move.
They’re most often found inert and covered
in dust, waiting for some energy source—
like a Stone Lord’s song or a thrumming
power-crystal—to give them “life.” They’ll
then follow any orders given to them in the
Stone Lord’s tongue.
A few of these servants are still active,
powered by inexhaustible sources in
ancient halls. Their joints are worn smooth,
their feet and hands ground down to nubs.
Some crawl on broken limbs, attempting
to complete tasks that will never end.
Stone sentinel
Solitary, large, construct, implacable
HP 24; Armor 3 (made of stone)
Damage crushing fists d10+3 (hand, close, grabby, messy, forceful, 3 piercing)
Special qualities can animate for only a few minutes at a time; dog-like mind; blind; tremorsense
Instinct to protect its ward
  • Emit an ominous hum, a warning as it powers up
  • Move with shocking, unexpected speed (but lose 1d4 HP)
  • Erupt in flurry of violence (area, +2 damage, lose 2d4 HP)

Articulated statues, squat and wide, with huge stone hands, set to watch forever over some ruin, passage, or treasure. Their stone “minds” are full of vigilance, and violence.

Each sentinel bears a power-crystal in its chest, slowly absorbing vibrations through earth and stone. A few days of charging is enough to animate the construct for only a few minutes—which is usually more than enough time for it to repel (or crush) an invader.

Mosaic swarm
Solitary, large, construct, amorphous,
stealthy
HP 24; Armor 3 (made of thousands of ceramic tiles)
Damage jagged fists of ceramic d10+3 (hand, close, forceful)
Special qualities externally powered; buglike mind; blind; tremorsense; unharmed by piercing weapons; half damage from cutting weapons; vulnerable to attacks that are blunt and forceful or area
Instinct to destroy intruders
  • Wait for intruders to get close, rattling softly
  • Explode into a cloud of whirling ceramic tiles, d8+1 damage (area, messy, 1 piercing)
  • Reform into a vaguely humanoid mass
Stone Lords adorned their halls with elab-
orate tilework and mosaics. Most of them
were decorative. Most of them.
Something interesting: Either some spirit
is possessing these tiles, or this is some sort
of construct. If it’s a spirit, there must be a
tether. If it’s a Stone Lord construct, well,
the individual tiles are too small to power
or direct themselves.
Something useful: The mosaic’s frame
remained on the wall, and it’s glowing
blue—aetherium runes! Destroy the runes,
and the construct should fall apart.
Pacifier
Group, small, construct, amorphous
HP 10; Armor 4 (just a stone sphere)
Damage stupefying pulse of sound d6 (reach, area, reload, ignores armor)
Special qualities must recharge after a few hours; limited autonomy; blind; tremorsense; has trouble with stairs; vulnerable to blunt, forceful attacks
Instinct to keep mortals docile
  • Sense and follow strong emotions
  • Hum a soothing, pacifying sound (near, area)
  • Sound the alarm, call for reinforcements
They’re basically hollow stone spheres,
maybe scratched and dinged but otherwise
featureless. Often found in their little
alcoves, charging. The Stone Lords would
deploy them among the rowdier servants
to keep things running smoothly.
When you are in range of a pacifier’s hum,
your emotions lose their edge. When you
rouse yourself to act violently, urgently, or
passionately, roll +WIS. If you are in range
of multiple pacifiers humming in unison,
you have disadvantage. On a 10+, you
shake off the effect; on a 7-9, you shake off
the effect but pick 1.
Your mind is addled; mark dazed
Your freedom is short-lived; the effect
returns if you pause for any reason
You get carried away or take things too
far, resulting in a consequence of the
GM’s choice
